courses.description: This course takes a deeper dive into American literature, featuring classic and modern works across a variety of genres. We will focus on developing your writing skills through four major projects: a literary analysis, an argumentative essay, a research paper, and a narrative piece. Along the way, you’ll strengthen your speaking, listening, and critical thinking skills.

This accelerated course is designed to prepare college-bound students for AP Language and/or AP Literature courses. Class size will be limited. Students will continue an intensive emphasis on the writing process in the informational and functional areas, as well as the continued development of listening, viewing and speaking skills. Students will study all genres of American literature, reading classic as well as modern American authors. Students must maintain a B in order to stay in the class.

Students read, write, listen to and view functional, informational and literary texts from different periods, cultures and genres in world literature. Students will also add to their presenting and speaking skills through group and individual practice.

Honors English 11 is designed to prepare college-bound students for AP Language and/or AP Literature courses. Students will read, write, listen to, view, and analyze functional, informational and literary texts from different periods, cultures and genres in world literature. Students will create and present group projects and presentations centered on world cultures and perspectives.

Students will read and write the kinds of discourse they will encounter during college and on the job. The course will focus on nonfiction literature, while preparing students for the AP Language and Composition exam. Discover why “success often comes to those who have the aptitude to see way down the road.”

courses.description: This course engages students in the careful reading and critical analysis of imaginative literature. Through close reading of selected literary works, students deepen their understanding and enhance their pleasure in literature. They develop critical standards for interpreting the effects writers create by means of the artful manipulation of language. To achieve these goals, students study individual works, their characters, action, structure, and language. They consider both large-scale literary elements such as form and theme, and smaller-scale elements such as figurative language, imagery, symbolism, and tone. Students analyze these and other aspects of literature in their need to derive meaning from their experience of reading. Students consider literary works in their historical context and in relation to their own lives and experience as well. Students must maintain a “B” each term to remain in class.

English 12 is a continuation of English 11 for seniors only. The class is designed to review and supplement previously taught writing skills, mechanical skills, and usage problems. The reading, which will include modern novels, is broad-based.

This course teaches rhetorical knowledge and skills, focusing on critical reading, writing, and thinking. Writing is emphasized as a process through multiple drafts and revisions. Coursework includes major essays, collaboration, research writing, journaling, and a portfolio.

Creative Writing is an intensive, portfolio-based class that will explore the various facets of creative writing. This class will cover topics such as short story writing, poetry, personal narrative writing, and novel writing.

Throughout the course students will dive into the qualities of dystopian literature and analyze how authors design that literature to address/critique certain social issues of their time. Students will be reading and analyzing multiple books in order to gain an in-depth understanding of the methods/strategies that authors use to tackle those issues, and further enhance their understanding of those methods/strategies through the development/creation of their own dystopia as the semester progresses.

Students will read and discuss four current, popular young adult (YA) novels. We will also consider why these books have gained popularity, who reads them, and where we think certain series are headed. (You will not have book reports for this course, but will need to read the novels to participate in class assignments and discussions.)

Science Fiction & Fantasy Literature looks at the history of science fiction and fantasy, current literature and what's trending. Students will read and discuss four popular science fiction and fantasy YA novels, create multiple projects to share what they are learning about both genres, and attend 1 or 2 field trips to extend the learning.

In this semester-long study of the genre, we will read, discuss, and analyze multiple contemporary and iconic Short Stories. We will dig into story elements, including setting, plot, characters, themes, symbols, sentence structure, and literary devices to understand how great stories are built. Your learning will culminate in you writing your own short stories by using the elements we have studied together.

This year-long course provides the study of and practice in gathering and analyzing information, interviewing, and note taking for the purpose of: (1) writing, (2) editing, and (3) publishing for the yearbook. Yearbook staff is responsible for recording the school year’s history in word and photography. Writers will work with the photographers to layout photos and words in order to create a technically sound publication. This course includes instruction and practice in effective journalistic writing forms and techniques as well as layout, design, photojournalism, and typography.

Ever wondered how movies shape the world? In this exciting course, students will dive into the power of film as both an art form and a force for change. From the early days of cinema to modern blockbusters, we'll explore how movies reflect and impact history, culture, and society. Students will watch, analyze, discuss and write about iconic films, gaining a deeper understanding of storytelling and filmmaking techniques. Beyond watching movies, students will also create their own short films, write scripts, and work on creative projects that bring their ideas to life.

If you love movies and want to take your film knowledge to the next level, this class is for you! Whether or not you’ve taken Film Lit 1, this course explores how different film genres—from suspenseful film noir to laugh-out-loud comedies—use editing, music, lighting, and camera work to tell powerful stories. Students will analyze groundbreaking films from around the world, create their own projects, and even try their hand at podcasting! This course is perfect for aspiring filmmakers, critics, or anyone who wants to see movies in a whole new way.

You'll analyze texts, respond to emails, write persuasive essays, engage in conversations, and give cultural presentations—all in French. By the end of the year, you'll be confident in expressing opinions, telling detailed stories, and discussing a variety of topics using connected sentences and short paragraphs. If you're serious about French and want to earn college credit, this is the course for you!

Students can earn additional college credits based on their performance on the AP test, with transfer credit information available at BYU AP Credit Transfer and UVU AP Credit Transfer. courses.description: Agricultural Biology is meant to be taken the entire year. This course is designed to meet the requirement for biological science credit. The standards and objectives for this course are the same as the standards and objectives for Biology, with the only difference being the degree of emphasis on agriculture. Students completing this course will be cognizant of current technologies, methods, and changes in agricultural science and are expected to know and apply the standards outlined in the core curriculum as they relate to the industry of agriculture.

Welcome to high school biology, where we dive into everything from cells’ wild drama (looking at you, cancer) to biochemistry that’s basically a high school party. One minute we’re watching protein synthesis team-building, the next we’re questioning if your cells are plotting a mutiny—spoiler: they probably are. We’ll also explore genetics and evolution, explaining everything from your brown eyes to your questionable dance moves, all tied to survival. And when we hit ecology, it’s like a reality show where organisms fight for food and survival. By the end, you might just rethink that last doughnut—nature’s a competitive game!

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit Option UVU: BIOL 1010 & BIOL 1015 lab (3 course credits \+ 1 lab credit) General Biology Course 1010 (3 college credits) Introduction to College Biology. Introduces major themes and concepts of biology including cell and molecular biology, genetics, diversity, evolution, and ecology. Provides students with necessary information and skills to critically evaluate what they hear, read, and see in the living world; communicate clearly; and apply methods to interpret data for making informed decisions concerning the role of biology in a world of which they are a part. General Biology Laboratory 1015 (1 college credit) Covers introductory topics in general biology. Complements the student's experience in the General Biology 1010 course with emphasis on the application of the scientific method. Includes actual student experiences with living organisms, use of the microscope, and an introduction to techniques used in the study of life.

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit Option UVU: BIOL 1610 & BIOL 1615 lab (4 course credits \+ 1 lab credit) Previous biology background is recommended before taking this course. This is a third level college biology course. If you are planning on entering the medical or science field for your job, this will be a course you take in college. It is for the highly motivated student who is passionate about biology. This class will engage you in science as a process. The informational component of the class will teach you how to collaborate with other students while communicating ideas and information learned about various topics. The laboratory component will enable you to develop hypotheses, design and run experiments, analyze data and make conclusions, and communicate in graphs and tables, but most importantly you will learn to think critically and analytically in the rapidly changing environment and societal world. The AP College Board outline will be followed. AP credit varies by school.

The Earth and Space Science SEEd Standards investigate processes and mechanisms that have resulted in the formation of our Earth, galaxy, and universe. Students develop models to illustrate the life span of the Sun and the role of nuclear fusion releasing energy in the Sun’s core. Students analyze and interpret data to construct an explanation for Earth’s history and explore changes to Earth’s systems. Students develop and use a model of Earth’s interior to describe the cycling of matter by thermal convection. Students plan and carry out an investigation on the properties of water to determine its effects on Earth materials. Students use computational thinking to explain sustainable and natural resources, focusing on responsible stewardship. Additionally, students design and evaluate solutions to problems that exist in these areas. Instruction will include labs, demonstrations, and research projects. Emphasis will also be placed on reading, writing, textbook assignments, vocabulary and correct spelling.

High school chemistry is like a rollercoaster through atoms, bonds, and the building blocks of the universe—minus the screams, replaced by groans over balancing equations. You’ll start with atoms (tiny, awkward Lego sets of protons, neutrons, and electrons) and then explore bonds—covalent, ionic, and metallic—like high school cliques, some friendly, some opposites, and some just sticking together because they’re bored. As the class moves on, expect chemical reactions that boom or fizzle, balancing equations like a puzzle master, and equilibrium, where things try to stay balanced but usually just get chaotic. Stoichiometry feels like math class in disguise, but you’ll survive the mole ratios. We’ll wrap it all up with nuclear chemistry, which is basically chemistry's way of saying, "I bet you didn’t think we’d go this far into the realm of the weird and radioactive."

Starting with atoms, the basic building blocks which make everything, and working to large molecules. The periodic table, states of matter and chemical bonding will be covered. Solutions and acid bases will also be discussed. Students will be involved in lab work. Chemistry is one of the fundamental sciences that teaches us about our surroundings.

Students study topics such as ecosystems, population growth, energy resources, climate change, pollution, and sustainable practices. Through labs, research, data interpretation, and real-world case studies, students learn to think scientifically and evaluate environmental challenges facing our world today. This course emphasizes problem-solving, critical thinking, and evidence-based decision-making, preparing students for the AP Environmental Science exam and/or concurrent enrollment college credit while building skills needed for future science and environmental careers.

Physics is the study of the “rules” of nature. An understanding of the way things work around us can increase our productivity as well as our enjoyment in life. The focus of this class is gaining an understanding of the concepts of Physics more than trying to do calculations. Some formulas and mathematical descriptions will be introduced and utilized to support the main concepts described. Instruction will include labs, demonstrations, and research projects. Emphasis will also be placed on reading, writing, textbook assignments, vocabulary, correct spelling and lab safety. Several units each term will cover topics such as science basics, properties of matter, forces, motion, energy, heat, electricity and magnetism, waves, sound, light, and the application of the various principles learned.

Prepare for the AP Physics Exam and the chance to earn college credit!

Students study concepts such as classes, methods, fields, data types, control constructs, data I/O, exception handling, and class libraries. courses.description: Animal Science is meant to be taken the entire year. This course exposes students to a wide range of scientific principles, such as genetics, anatomy, physiology/nutrition, disease, pests, and management practices. The science processes of observation, measurement, hypothesizing, data gathering, interpretation, analysis, and application are stressed. Career opportunities and educational preparation are examined. Learning activities are varied, with classroom and laboratory experiences emphasized.

This intro to astronomy gives students a broad understanding of the night sky, from ancient theories to modern discoveries. Dive into planets, stars, black holes, rockets, constellations, and more through projects, activities, and presentations. Learn how to track the moon, use the stars to tell time, and explore constellations. When possible, we’ll head outside with telescopes to see planets, stars, and the moon up close!

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit UVU: 1040 An introductory course in astronomy with the goal to provide students with a broad enough, deep enough background in astronomy to appreciate the night sky and understand the motions, properties and history of the heavenly objects. Students are introduced to astronomy through the eyes of ancient philosophers, scientists and mathematicians. The curriculum then escalates through modern theories and ideas, observations and discoveries. Each planet, the birth and death of stars, constellations, comets, asteroids, back holes, etc. are each discussed in detail.

Welcome to high school genetics, where we explore everything that makes you… well, you! We start with Mendelian genetics—think of dominant and recessive genes as the pushy older sibling vs. the quiet younger one. Then we dive into non-Mendelian genetics, where things get wild with incomplete dominance, co-dominance, and epistasis. You’ll even debate whether your last tomato secretly has fish DNA. Next, we tackle DNA, cloning, and stem cells. DNA is your body’s instruction manual (if you can read nerdy code), and cloning? It’s not about an army of sheep… maybe. Stem cell therapy could one day fix your organs—and cure the common cold (fingers crossed). Finally, we’ll talk epigenetics—like the DJ of your genes, telling them when to turn on or off based on your lifestyle. Get ready to leave class feeling like a genetic genius.

This course prepares students to care for horses and horse equipment; to train horses for various work and athletic or entertainment roles; and to manage horse training, breeding, and housing programs and facilities. Students will also cover horse sciences which include genetics, anatomy, physiology/nutrition, diseases, and pests. Learning activities are varied, with classroom, laboratory, and field experiences emphasized to care for horses and horse equipment.

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit Option UVU: GEO 1010 & GEO 1015 lab Geology is the study of the Earth, its systems, characteristics, and place in the solar system. The focus in this class is on the importance of geology in human affairs and how geologic systems control the physical setting of our environment. Instruction will include labs, demonstrations, and research projects. Emphasis will also be placed on reading, writing, textbook assignments, vocabulary and correct spelling. Several units each term will be covered on the topics of space, earth structure, earth systems, plate tectonics, minerals, igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks, geologic time, seismic activity, weathering, mass movement, rivers, ground-water, glaciers shorelines, deserts, ocean basins, continents, resources, and the application of the various principles learned. Field trips will be taken during class time to several local locations where students can see examples of concepts covered and do work using geological tools and procedures.

The course is designed for students with an interest in marine biology and oceanography. This course provides an excellent background for students who are interested in further study of the oceans and the organisms that inhabit it. Major concepts include the study of: the interrelationship of marine and terrestrial environments, the geology of the oceans, marine organisms, ecology of coral reefs, and the human impact on marine ecosystems. Laboratory activities, including the examination of marine specimens, are utilized throughout this course to build upon student knowledge.

Are you interested in learning more about the amazing human body? Is a career in the medical field a possible option for your future? Do you want to learn anatomy and physiology through hands-on activities? Do you want a class that will prepare you for college level courses? Then this class is for you! Students will also be engaged in dissections, medical career exploration, field trips, and much more. Students will examine each of the twelve body systems at a level of detail that will allow them to pass college level courses in the same areas of study. This class is a must for any students pursuing medical related careers such as medicine, dentistry, physical therapy, massage therapy, sports medicine, nursing and many more.

courses.description: Plant and Soil Science is meant to be taken the entire year. This year long course is Concurrent Enrollment eligible through Utah State. This course will expose students to a wide range of plant, landscape, and greenhouse operation and management practices. Students will learn about the anatomy and plant production processes. This will prepare students to produce commercial plant species in a natural and controlled environment, as well as manage commercial and experimental operations.

Welcome to high school wildlife biology, where you’ll explore biomes from the tundra to the rainforest and learn how animals survive in their unique homes. We’ll cover limiting factors (like food and water) that decide who thrives, and dive into ecological drama like predator-prey and symbiosis (nature’s “you scratch my back” moment). Plus, you’ll master the art of taxonomy, where animals get their fancy Latin names. But it’s not all fun and games—there are invasive species causing chaos, and you’ll learn how to measure animal populations without counting every critter. We’ll also dive into biodiversity and how human actions are putting it at risk. By the end, you’ll appreciate the delicate balance of ecosystems and hopefully feel a little better about the wild things around us.

Students will study the diversity of animal life. They will analyze the hierarchical organization of animal complexity and contrast this to the classification of animal species. Students will explore the great diversity of animal species beginning with simple invertebrate animals up through the complex vertebrates. The principles of natural selection will be reviewed in order to evaluate the anatomical features of animals and how species physically adapt to their environment in order to survive. The detailed behavior of animals including learning, dominance, and communication will be explored. Current global environmental trends that threaten the survival of species will be addressed. Hands-on, student-centered activities will allow students to become more aware of the fascinating science of zoology.

Students in Secondary 1 will deepen and extend understanding of linear relationships, in part by contrasting with exponential phenomena, and in part by applying linear models to data that exhibit a linear trend. Students will also use the properties and theorems involving congruent figures to deepen and extend their understanding of geometric knowledge. Algebraic and geometric ideas are tied together. Students will experience mathematics as a coherent, useful, and logical subject that makes use of their ability to make sense of problem situations.

Students in Secondary 2 will deepen their understanding of geometric properties to include proofs involving triangles, quadrilaterals, similarity, and circles. Students will also be introduced to trigonometric ratios and use them to solve real world problems. Significant time will be spent on analyzing quadratic functions and their applications. Students will work with polynomials, radicals, and complex numbers, which will allow them to solve quadratic equations using various methods. Students will explore transformations of functions using graphs and equations. Students will also solve problems involving compound and conditional probability.

Secondary Math 2 Extended is an accelerated course for students on the track toward AP Calculus. The curriculum primarily focuses on quadratic expressions, equations, and functions, including transformations and comparisons to linear and exponential models. Students extend the number system to complex numbers to solve all quadratic equations. The course continues with rigorous geometric proofs, similarity, right-triangle trigonometry, and circles. Specific extended topics include conic sections and calculating probabilities using permutations and combinations. This fast-paced course emphasizes the mathematical reasoning and procedural fluency required for success in advanced, upper-level AP coursework.

Secondary Math 3 builds on Secondary Math 1 and 2, deepening students' understanding of algebra, functions, geometry, and statistics while emphasizing reasoning, problem-solving, and real-world applications. Students will extend their knowledge of linear, quadratic, and exponential functions to include polynomial, rational, and logarithmic functions, apply trigonometry, and analyze data using probability and statistics.

Students in Secondary Mathematics III will pull together and apply the learning that they have from their previous courses. They will apply methods from probability and statistics to draw inferences and conclusions from data. Students expand their repertoire of functions to include polynomial, rational, and radical functions. They will expand their study of right triangle trigonometry to include general triangles. Honors students will also use probability to evaluate decisions, expand their knowledge of polynomial and trigonometric identities, and perform algebraic operations on rational expressions.

Secondary Math 3 builds on Secondary Math 1 and 2, deepening students' understanding of algebra, functions, geometry, and statistics while emphasizing reasoning, problem-solving, and real-world applications. Students will extend their knowledge of linear, quadratic, and exponential functions to include polynomial, rational, and logarithmic functions, apply trigonometry, and analyze data using probability and statistics.

Students in Secondary Mathematics III will pull together and apply the learning that they have from their previous courses. They will apply methods from probability and statistics to draw inferences and conclusions from data. Students expand their repertoire of functions to include polynomial, rational, and radical functions. They will expand their study of right triangle trigonometry to include general triangles. Honors students will also use probability to evaluate decisions, expand their knowledge of polynomial and trigonometric identities, and perform algebraic operations on rational expressions.

This course will develop skills in Data Science and statistics by emphasizing a modeling approach. Students will reason and make sense of data using the Statistical Problem Solving Process consisting of: 1\) Formulating Statistical Investigative Questions, 2\) Collect/Consider Data, 3\) Analyze Data, and 4\) Interpreting the Results. Students will learn to use data and modeling to formulate and answer questions as well as to critically evaluate information using mathematics as the reasoning tool. The class will use the R computer coding language and Jupyter notebooks - two tools used by actual data scientists in the real world. This course is an equivalent replacement for secondary math 3\. Students could take AP Statistics or Math 1010 after this course.

courses.description: AP Precalculus is a year-long, college-level mathematics course designed to prepare students for calculus and other STEM-related disciplines. It focuses on developing a deep conceptual understanding of functions as models of dynamic phenomena and building procedural and symbolic fluency. The main topics of focus are Polynomial and Rational Functions, Exponential and Logarithmic Functions, Trigonometric and Polar Functions, as well as Functions Involving Parameters, Vectors, and Matrices

metadata.optional_fee: $99 to take spring AP test, due in October

courses.description: Get ready to dive into the world of calculus—the language of motion, change, and everything in between! AP Calculus AB isn’t just a class; it’s an adventure into the heart of mathematics. You’ll tackle limits, conquer derivatives, and master integrals, all while discovering how calculus shapes the real world—from physics to economics to engineering. This course is fast-paced, problem-packed, and designed to sharpen your critical thinking. You’ll explore functions graphically, numerically, and algebraically, using technology to unlock deeper insights. Along the way, you’ll develop the mathematical reasoning skills needed for college and beyond. By the end of the course, you'll be ready to take on the AP exam and demonstrate your mastery of calculus. Whether you’re aiming for college credit or just want to challenge yourself with higher-level math, this course will push you to think in new ways and build a strong foundation for future success.

metadata.optional_fee: $99 to take spring AP test, due in October

courses.description: AP Calculus BC is a rigorous, college-level mathematics course that is the equivalent of both first and second-semester college calculus. The course builds upon the foundational concepts of AP Calculus AB and extends them to more advanced topics, with an emphasis on a multi-representational approach (graphical, numerical, analytical, and verbal) to problem-solving. The primary goal is to develop a deep understanding of calculus concepts and provide experience with methods and applications, preparing students for further study in mathematics, engineering, physics, computer science, and economics. Key objectives include: Cultivating an understanding of limits, derivatives, integrals, and infinite sequences and series. Applying definitions and theorems to build mathematical arguments and justify conclusions. Connecting concepts across different representations to understand how calculus applies to various functions and real-world problems. Using technology (graphing calculators) to explore concepts, confirm work, and interpret results.

metadata.optional_fee: $99 to take spring AP test, due in October

courses.description: Unlock the power of data analysis in this engaging advanced placement course! You’ll learn how to collect, summarize, and analyze data to uncover patterns, spot trends, and make predictions about future behavior. Dive deep into probability, counting theory, simulations, and mathematical modeling with a hands-on approach to solving real-world problems. Technology will be your tool for creating simulations and analyzing data, helping you develop critical thinking and decision-making skills. A graphing calculator (TI-83+ or any TI-84 recommended) is required to fully engage with the course content. Get ready to explore the fascinating world of data and predictions!

courses.description: \\Full year can give UVU: CS 1400 Credit “I think everybody in this country should learn how to program a computer because it teaches you how to think,” said Steve Jobs. This year-long computer programming course will be fun and also teach you to think. This includes creating software, game development, and business applications. Students will learn to design, code, and test their own programs. Students will learn Microsoft’s C\# programming language, Kodu game development, and MonoGame studio. As a supplement and reinforcement, students will create basic PC games. This course includes the fundamentals and basic theories of computer programming, simple control and data structures, basic operating systems and file commands, and the use of text files. Students will also apply mathematical skills throughout the course. The course fundamentals include conditionals, looping, methods, file manipulation, arrays, objects, and classes.

Parent signed math opt out form required How can mathematical reasoning help you navigate adulthood? Math for Decision Making is a practical course designed to improve numerical literacy and equip you with skills for well-informed decision-making. Throughout the year, you will explore personal finance (taxes, budgeting, loans), data literacy (probability, statistical bias), and civic mathematics (voting systems, election fairness). You will also use technology to model real-world scenarios like population growth and applied geometry. Because the four quarters are independent, students may enter or exit the course at the start of any quarter. This course provides the foundational numerical literacy and strategic tools needed to use math with confidence in your daily life.

This course is designed to cover the history of the United States from 1865 to the present. Students will study the social, economic, and political developments of this period. Emphasis will be placed on events, issues and the men and women important to our national heritage. There is also an in depth study of the constitution and how it works.

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit UVU: HIST 1700 Welcome to HIST 1700: American Civilization/AP U.S. History, a college-level course that dives into the history, culture, and development of the United States from its founding to the present. This course challenges you to think deeply, analyze complex ideas, and engage in meaningful discussions about the events and movements that have shaped American society. Expect that this college-level course will hold the same expectations as if you were a student on campus at UVU, which means you'll read a lot, write a lot, and be required to think critically about events and issues, while developing skills that will serve you far beyond this class. Together, we'll explore topics like democracy, civil rights, and cultural shifts, always asking how America's past continues to impact its present and future. Be ready to step up, speak up, and grow as a thoughtful student of history and society. Students in this class also have the option to take the AP U.S. History exam in the Spring, and will have opportunities to prepare for that throughout the year. This means that you can potentially earn up to 6 college credits (3 from UVU and 3 from AP).
This course will give students a general understanding of the government, political and legal systems of the United States and the responsibilities of being a good citizen.

metadata.fee: There is a tuition charge of $5 per college credit for all concurrent enrollment classes – payable to the respective university. (Example: 3 credit hour classes would cost $15).

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit UVU: POLS 1100 In CE Government and Citizenship students will learn the foundations of American government and how the political process works. Students who take an active approach in their learning will not only understand the history, institutions, and actors in American politics, but they will also be able to critically analyze current political issues. We will explore the political structures (legislative, executive, judicial, and bureaucratic), behaviors, and processes through which we are governed.

The course will provide students with an in depth study of the government, political and legal systems of the United States. Practical learning skills will be combined with the instruction from outside resource persons and varied activities which will enable students to learn about law and the legal system of this nation. Students may receive college credit and are expected to take the AP exam in May. Students passing the AP exam with a score of 3 or above will receive college credit.

courses.description: A thematically based course that will cover the progress of civilization from its beginnings around 3500 B.C. to the advent of Medieval times approximately 500 A.D. The main goal of this course is to show how the past has influenced the present. To do this the class will focus on social, economic and political issues throughout many of the early, ancient civilizations such as Mesopotamia, Egypt, Greece, Rome, China, and India.

metadata.fee: There is a tuition charge of $5 per college credit for all concurrent enrollment classes – payable to the respective university. (Example: 3 credit hour classes would cost $15).

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit Option UVU: ART 1010 How do we understand art in our world? What is the function of art now and how does that compare to how art was understood by past peoples? This course develops an appreciation of the visual arts by investigating the elements and principles of art, art criticism, art production, and the history of art. Students identify works of art and describe their significance in writing. You will define (and re-define) art and understand its changing relationship with society over the centuries. Class discussions will be critical to the learning process. Students are expected to look at art, read, think, write, ask, share observations, and discuss.

In this class, you'll dive into the latest news and learn how to think critically about what's happening around the world. You'll explore different sources to understand big global issues. By discussing with classmates, you'll learn to see things from different angles and form your own opinions. This course will help you become more informed and engaged with the world today.

Ever wondered why prices go up, how businesses make money, or why some countries are richer than others? This course dives into real-world economic issues and how societies tackle them. You'll explore how the U.S. economy works, its role in the global market, and—most importantly—how you can use economic principles in your own life to make smarter financial decisions.

metadata.optional_fee: $99 to take spring AP test, due in October

courses.description: AP Economics is a full-year, college-level course that covers both AP Microeconomics and AP Macroeconomics. Students will study how individuals, businesses, and governments make decisions about scarce resources and how markets and economies function. The course includes analysis of supply and demand, market structures, economic indicators, fiscal and monetary policy, and international trade. Emphasis is placed on interpreting graphs and data, applying economic reasoning to real-world situations, and preparing students for the AP Microeconomics and AP Macroeconomics exams.

Get ready to rock! In this engaging and dynamic course, students will explore the rich history of rock and roll music, from its roots in blues, gospel, and country to its role as a driving force in social, cultural, and political movements. Along the way, students will analyze key artists, influential albums, and major technological innovations that shaped the genre and left a lasting impact on American and global culture. Through listening exercises, discussions, multimedia analysis, and creative projects, students will gain a deeper understanding of the evolution of rock music and its intersections with history, fashion, technology, and identity. From Elvis Presley and The Beatles to Nirvana and modern alternative rock, this course offers something for every music lover.

courses.description: This course will track and discuss the progression of world events from the 1200’s A.D. Starting with Medieval times then transitioning into the dawn of Modern History with the Age of Exploration and Renaissance. We will discuss the impact of the Enlightenment, Age of Revolution, Age of Imperialism, and the World Wars Era, then end with the Cold War Era. Through the course of the units listed above this class will study how the many different peoples of the world have intermingled, affected each other, and influenced the way the world is today.

From the rise of rock ‘n’ roll to the impact of social media, pop culture has shaped the way we think, dress, and connect with the world. This course explores the history of music, film, television, fashion, and technology, revealing how cultural movements reflect and influence society. Through discussions, media analysis, and hands-on projects, students will uncover the deeper meanings behind their favorite trends and discover how pop culture has driven social change throughout history. If you love the world of entertainment—or just want to understand the forces shaping our own—this class is for you!

Ever wondered why people think, feel, and act the way they do? Dive into the fascinating world of psychology, where science meets the mysteries of the human mind! In this course, you'll explore everything from brain chemistry to personality, dreams to decision-making, and even the power of social influence. Through hands-on activities, real-world case studies, and mind-blowing experiments (some of which you will conduct yourself), you’ll gain insights into what makes us tick and be able to see the world through a whole new lens.

metadata.fee: There is a tuition charge of $5 per college credit for all concurrent enrollment classes – payable to the respective university. (Example: 3 credit hour classes would cost $15).

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit UVU: PSY 1010 Step into the world of psychology at the college level and earn credit while exploring the science behind human thought and behavior! This UVU-partnered course delves into brain function, cognition, mental health, social dynamics, and psychological research methods. Through in-depth discussions, case studies, and hands-on experiments, students will develop critical thinking skills and apply psychological principles to real-world situations. Whether you're preparing for a future in psychology or just curious about what makes people tick, this course challenges you to think deeper, analyze data, and gain insights that will stick with you long after the semester ends!

metadata.optional_fee: $99 to take spring AP test, due in October

courses.description: This class is taught in a facilitator-run classroom with a remote teacher. The AP Psychology course is designed to prepare students for the Advanced Placement Program examination. The course emphasizes the history of psychology as a science. The classes examine the various theories that form the foundation of human and animal behavior. The lessons trace the development of the major schools of psychology. The scientific nature of psychology is examined by means of experimental research methodology and inferential statistics. Fundamental areas of exploration include: the biological basis of behavior, sensation and perception, psychological disorders, states of consciousness, learning, personality, emotional, abnormal psychology, and ethics.

Sports History explores the evolution of sports and their impact on culture, society, and politics. Students will study the origins of various sports, the role of athletes, and how events have shaped global and local communities. Through discussions and projects, students will analyze the influence of sports on race, gender, economics, and social change.

In Sociology, you’ll explore how society shapes who we are and how we see the world. We’ll look at real-life topics like social media, peer pressure, family life, inequality, culture, dating, values and social change. We'll look at human behaviors and social interactions and what they teach us about ourselves. You’ll analyze how schools, communities, and cultures influence everyday life—and how individuals and groups fit into them. If you like asking why things are the way they are and talking about real-world issues that matter, this class is for you.

This course will take an in depth look into the time periods of World War I and World War II. We will study the causes and effects of both wars as well as delving into specific campaigns and personal accounts of each war.

From Egypt's first woman pharaoh to today's female presidents and prime ministers, women have played a major role in history. This course will offer a survey of women in America, the role of gender in American politics, and the experiences of women from different classes and races.

metadata.optional_fee: $99 to take spring AP test, due in October

courses.description: This course is designed for those who desire to continue their education beyond high school. The course is an in-depth study of World History from 1000 C.E. to the present. Practical learning skills will be combined with essay writing to analyze events which will enable students to learn about the world around them. Students taking this class are encouraged to take the AP exam in May. Students passing the AP exam with a score of 3 or above will likely receive college credit depending on their choice of college.

World Religions is a comparative course designed to help students gain a better understanding of, and an appreciation for, a variety of religious beliefs, traditions, and practices around the world, thereby helping students develop a general sense of religious tolerance and respect. The course will compare and contrast the major religions of the world, including Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, and Judaism. We will also look into a variety of lesser known religious beliefs and practices.

Grades 10-12 Dance clothes required Dance 1 explores a variety of dance styles. You’ll learn fun choreography, build skill and confidence, and make friends as you perform together. You’ll experience music and movement, and learn to use time, space, and energy to create dance. A performance at the end of the semester is required.

Grades 10-12 Dance clothes required Dance 2 builds on Dance 1\. You’ll continue learning choreography, keep building dance and performance skills, and get more performance opportunities. You’ll also increase your ability to create dance as an artist. A performance at the end of the semester is required.

courses.description: Grades 10-12 Dance clothes required Dance Company is a full-year, advanced level dance company. Company members will gain broadened perspectives, as well as increase in technical mastery, choreographic and improvisational skill, and performance artistry. While Dance Company performs at a variety of events throughout the year, including halftimes and assemblies, emphasis is placed on the production of an artistic concert performance. Students must be committed to attending extra-curricular rehearsals and performances, especially the semester-end concerts.

courses.description: Dance clothes required Are you ready to take your dance skills to the next level? The Ballroom Company is a competitive performance opportunity, focused on learning and mastering both individual partner and formation routines in a variety of ballroom styles. Students should be dedicated, disciplined, and prepared to commit to a competitive dance team experience.

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit UVU: THEA 1013 Step into the spotlight! Drama 3 is an exciting, hands-on course where students will develop their acting skills, experiment with playwriting, and create their own theatrical performances. Through character development, improvisation, Shakespeare, and ensemble work, students will grow their confidence both on and off the stage. Drama 3 students will perform in the Night of Shakespeare and the Night of One Acts and will compete at both the Region and State Drama Competitions. Whether you're an experienced performer or just looking to build confidence, this class will challenge and inspire you. Optional college credit available for THEA 1013, Introduction to Theatre, with registration through UVU.

metadata.fee: $60.00, (Uniform, Other fees may apply.) See Drama spend plan for additional fees.

courses.description: Ready to take your acting skills to the next level? Drama 4 is an advanced performance course where students will refine their craft through challenging acting techniques, ensemble work, and experimental theatre. Students will dive deep into comedy and tragedy, master Shakespearean performance, and explore cutting-edge acting styles. This class isn’t just about performing—it’s about creating meaningful, powerful theatre that resonates with audiences. Drama 4 students will produce a mainstage play and compete at the Utah Shakespearean Festival as well as Region and State Drama Competitions.

metadata.fee: $60.00, (Uniform, Other fees may apply.) See Drama spend plan for additional fees.

courses.description: Love singing, dancing, and acting? This high-energy course is for students who want to bring musical theatre to life! In this class, students will train in vocal performance, choreography, and acting techniques while preparing for the school’s fall and spring musical productions. Students will develop skills in auditioning, character work, and performance, working both in class and after school to create professional-quality productions. But this class is about more than just performing—it’s about building confidence, teamwork, communication, and discipline. Students will develop skills that go far beyond the stage, such as problem-solving, collaboration, time management, and resilience. Whether they go on to pursue a career in the arts or not, Musical Dance Theatre gives students the ability to express themselves, work effectively in a team, and present themselves with confidence in any setting. If you dream of performing or just want to challenge yourself in a supportive and creative environment, this class is the perfect opportunity!

courses.description: Ever wondered how the magic of theatre happens behind the scenes? This hands-on course introduces students to the essential skills of technical theatre, including set design, lighting, sound, costume construction, and stage makeup. Students will build and create props, costumes, and scenery, learning how to bring a production to life. Whether you're interested in theatre tech, design, or just want to pick up some incredible hands-on skills, this class will give you real-world experience in the behind-the-scenes magic of theatre! Required after school hours: 1st semester 20 hours, 2nd semester 30 hours

Students will design and implement technical elements for productions, gaining real-world experience that prepares them for university theatre programs or careers in the entertainment industry. Stage Crew members also run the school auditorium, ensuring that productions and events run smoothly. If you love problem-solving, working with your hands, and bringing productions to life, this class is for you!

courses.description: Ever wondered how movies shape the world? In this exciting course, students will dive into the power of film as both an art form and a force for change. From the early days of cinema to modern blockbusters, we'll explore how movies reflect and impact history, culture, and society. Students will watch, analyze, discuss and write about iconic films, gaining a deeper understanding of storytelling and filmmaking techniques. Beyond watching movies, students will also create their own short films, write scripts, and work on creative projects that bring their ideas to life.

If you love movies and want to take your film knowledge to the next level, this class is for you! Whether or not you’ve taken Film Lit 1, this course explores how different film genres—from suspenseful film noir to laugh-out-loud comedies—use editing, music, lighting, and camera work to tell powerful stories. Students will analyze groundbreaking films from around the world, create their own projects, and even try their hand at podcasting! This course is perfect for aspiring filmmakers, critics, or anyone who wants to see movies in a whole new way.

courses.description: This beginner art course explores the basics of art—like line, shape, color, and texture—through fun projects and different materials. You'll boost your creativity, critical thinking, and teamwork skills while working on assignments using different art mediums (like paint, color pencil, graphite). Plus, you'll learn how to take care of the studio and art supplies.

metadata.fee: $40.00 Semester

courses.description: Ceramics 1 is messy, fun and for all skill levels with varied experience. The class is an introduction to beginning wheel throwing and hand-building techniques incorporating surface design and glazing on each project. Emphasis is placed on developing creativity, critical thinking, communication and collaboration skills. Participating in all activities including daily care and maintenance of the studio, clay tools, and equipment is required. No prior knowledge of clay or ceramics is needed. Consistent, in person attendance is required.

metadata.fee: $40.00 Semester

courses.description: Ceramics 2 takes your pottery skills to the next level with a strong focus on wheel throwing! You’ll refine the techniques learned in previous ceramic classes while also exploring creative surface design. Beyond making cool pottery, this class helps you sharpen your creativity, critical thinking, and collaboration skills. Plus, you'll be part of a hands-on studio environment where everyone pitches in to keep the space, tools, and equipment in top shape. This class is for students who have completed high school Ceramics 1 and is in-person only—because you can’t throw clay over Zoom!

metadata.fee: $40.00 Semester

courses.description: Ready to take your ceramics skills to the next level? This advanced studio course gives you the freedom to develop your own ideas and push your limits in clay. You’ll refine your wheel-throwing, hand-building, and learn new firing techniques while exploring ways to bring your creative vision to life. With a strong focus on the artistic process, you’ll plan, experiment, and execute original projects, building on everything you learned in Ceramics 1 & 2\. Want to create an AP Art Portfolio? You’ll have the option to develop one and take the AP test. This hands-on, in-person course is designed for dedicated ceramic artists ready to challenge themselves. If you love working with clay and want to elevate your craft, this is the class for you! In-person attendance required.

In this class, you'll experiment with a variety of materials and sculpture techniques, learning how to transform simple concepts into amazing 3D forms. From sketching out ideas to studying inspiring artists, you’ll discover the unique possibilities of each medium while developing your own artistic style. This course isn’t just about making cool art—it’s about pushing your creativity, problem-solving, and collaboration skills to the next level. You'll be actively engaged in every step of the process, from brainstorming to building, all while helping maintain a working studio space. Open to all skill levels, this in-person class is a must for anyone ready to get their hands dirty and their creativity flowing! The course fee includes all materials (3 different types of clay, foam, mixed media, tools, cleaning supplies, cold finish supplies, and various adhesives).

Whether you're a beginner or a budding artist, you’ll master essential techniques like coil, slab, and pinch while experimenting with texture, form, and surface design. From sketching ideas to bringing them to life in 3D, you'll push your creativity to new heights. But this class is more than just making cool sculptures—it’s about thinking outside the box, problem-solving, and working together. You’ll share ideas, give feedback, and collaborate in a dynamic studio environment where everyone helps keep the space running. No experience needed—just bring your imagination! You will leave this course with many of your own paintings in a variety of sizes and styles.

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit Option UVU: ART 1010 How do we understand art in our world? What is the function of art now and how does that compare to how art was understood by past peoples? This course develops an appreciation of the visual arts by investigating the elements and principles of art, art criticism, art production, and the history of art. Students identify works of art and describe their significance in writing. You will define (and re-define) art and understand its changing relationship with society over the centuries. Class discussions will be critical to the learning process. Students are expected to look at art, read, think, write, ask, share observations, and discuss.

In this course, students explore how to bring ideas to life through visual design using digital tools (Adobe Suite) and creative problem-solving. Students create eye-catching posters, logos, and digital graphics while building strong skills in typography, color, composition, and essential design principles.

Ever wanted to take cool photos but don’t know where to start? Whether you want to capture fun moments or dream of becoming a photographer, this is the perfect place to start.

Note: Students will be required to have access to a film SLR camera and a Digital camera. Now it’s time to take your photography further. If you’re thinking about a career in photography or just want to be amazing at it, this class will get you there!

As a key part of the yearbook team, you’ll go beyond the basics—capturing high-quality, professional-level images that showcase the best moments of the school year. You’ll learn advanced camera techniques, composition, lighting, and action photography to make every shot count. Plus, you’ll master photo editing, layout design, and visual storytelling to create pages that are eye-catching and memorable. This class is perfect for students who are ready to push their creativity, meet real deadlines, and produce a yearbook that truly stands out!

courses.description: This dynamic, high school Health II course is your essential guide to navigating life's challenges and building a powerful foundation for lifelong success. We move beyond basic facts to focus on developing the crucial skills for balanced well-being across the entire health triangle: Physical, Mental/Emotional, and Social Health. You will learn practical, life-saving skills like CPR and First Aid, while developing lifelong strategies for sound nutrition, fitness, and overall disease prevention. Dive deep into self-awareness, applying stress management techniques and developing crucial resiliency skills to cope with life changes and explore community mental health resources. Finally, you will build and model effective communication, decision-making, and refusal skills to maintain healthy relationships, set personal boundaries , and resist negative peer pressure, especially concerning substance abuse. This course is dedicated to giving you the knowledge and confident behaviors needed to establish a healthy, safe lifestyle and thrive throughout your lifespan.

Coursework stresses the safe and skillful use of millworking machines, portable power tools and hand tools in the construction of fine furniture. Projects may include, but are not limited to chests of drawers, dressers, tables, entertainment centers, hutches, gun cabinets, cedar chests and beds.

This course exposes students to a wide range of scientific principles, such as genetics, anatomy, physiology/nutrition, disease, pests, and management practices. The science processes of observation, measurement, hypothesizing, data gathering, interpretation, analysis, and application are stressed. Career opportunities and educational preparation are examined. Learning activities are varied, with classroom, laboratory, and field experiences emphasized.

courses.description: Agricultural Biology is meant to be taken the entire year. This course is designed to meet the requirement for biological science credit. The standards and objectives for this course are the same as the standards and objectives for Biology, with the only difference being the degree of emphasis on agriculture. Students completing this course will be cognizant of current technologies, methods, and changes in agricultural science and are expected to know and apply the standards outlined in the core curriculum as they relate to the industry of agriculture.

courses.description: This course covers principles of floral art with an emphasis on commercial design. Topics include basic design styles and color harmonies; identification, use, and care of processing of cut flowers and foliage; mechanical aids and containers; personal flowers; holiday designs; and plant identification and care. The student will demonstrate the ability to identify floral design styles and color harmonies; identify cut flowers and foliage and the care and processing methods for extended vase life; select containers and mechanical aids; and create basic floral arrangements. The study of the general principles of plants, and their life processes and morphology, with emphasis on major floral crops are also covered.

courses.description: Plant and Soil Science is meant to be taken the entire year. This year long course is Concurrent Enrollment eligible through Utah State. This course will expose students to a wide range of plant, landscape, and greenhouse operation and management practices. Students will learn about the anatomy and plant production processes. This will prepare students to produce commercial plant species in a natural and controlled environment, as well as manage commercial and experimental operations.

This course prepares students to care for horses and horse equipment; to train horses for various work and athletic or entertainment roles; and to manage horse training, breeding, and housing programs and facilities. Students will also cover horse sciences which include genetics, anatomy, physiology/nutrition, diseases, and pests. Learning activities are varied, with classroom, laboratory, and field experiences emphasized to care for horses and horse equipment.

This course provides the opportunity for students to explore different avenues of the veterinary profession. Students will be exposed to veterinary science and principles which include anatomy, physiology, chemistry, animal health and disease, dentistry and laboratory procedures. Students will provide hands-on care as they develop skills in the areas of surgical assisting, bandaging, wound care, oral care, and general nursing care.

This one-of-a-kind course provides students with real-world insights into the dynamic real estate industry. Throughout the semester, students will explore: The scope and significance of the real estate industry. Forces shaping the land market and its unique characteristics. Various uses of land and types of property ownership. Key aspects of contracts, deeds, and the processes involved in buying and selling real estate. Mortgages and financing methods for real estate investments. Understanding the short-term and long-term costs associated with financing. Strategies for advertising and promotional activities within the real estate sector. …and so much more! This course equips students with in-depth knowledge and practical skills to spark a future interest in real estate, whether as an investor, homeowner, or industry professional.

courses.description: This course introduces the processes and activities involved in business. The course provides core content applicable to all aspects of business and encompasses the practical applications of management theory. Students will be introduced to the fundamental management functions including planning, organizing, leading and controlling from multiple perspectives. This course is designed with a skill based approach and focuses on: communication (oral, written, nonverbal and listening), problem solving/ethical reasoning, teamwork, decision making, conflict resolution, critical analysis.

courses.description: Marketing 1 explores the basic concepts of marketing and advertising. Students will create campaigns to promote items or movements of their own choosing as well as participating in group projects, all with the goal of learning by doing. Marketing includes planning, target market identification, research, pricing strategies, promotion, and more. Everything in life is related to marketing and you will never wonder if you will ever use the material we learn in class. Students will also participate in DECA.

Want to learn how brands, influencers, and businesses grow online? Digital Marketing teaches students how to promote products, services, and personal brands using today’s most powerful online tools. In this course, students learn how companies use social media, content creation, influencers, and digital advertising to reach customers. Students will analyze real brands, create their own marketing campaigns, and learn how to turn creativity into real-world skills. Students will learn to: Create and manage social media content Understand branding and target audiences Explore influencer marketing and monetization Analyze digital ads and marketing data Build basic marketing strategies used by real businesses Perfect for students interested in: Social media & content creation Business, entrepreneurship, or e-commerce Graphic design, video, or creative marketing Running a personal brand or online business

Ever thought about owning a business or becoming a leader? Business Management teaches students how businesses are started, organized, and successfully run. Students will explore how companies make decisions, manage money, lead teams, and solve real-world problems. Through hands-on projects, students learn how to think like business owners and managers. Students will learn to: Understand how businesses operate day-to-day Learn leadership and decision-making skills Explore entrepreneurship and business ownership Manage budgets, operations, and teams Analyze real business case studies Perfect for students interested in: Entrepreneurship and leadership Management or business careers Finance, operations, or real estate Running their own company someday

courses.description: Entrepreneurship is a semester class which introduces students to the perils, pitfalls and joys of small business ownership. Students will explore the traits of successful entrepreneurs and the important role they play in our economy. The entire process of starting a small business from initial research to management and legal concerns will be examined. Additional topics include innovation, patents and copyrights, entity structuring, financing and marketing. Students will be guided through the process of developing a complete business plan.

This course seeks to introduce students to the specialized marketing activities involved in recreation and sporting events. Students will explore concepts such as recreation and sporting events and competitions, sponsorship, merchandising, integrated marketing campaigns, promotional activities, and calendars. Students should be introduced to the CTSOs for this course including DECA and FBLA and the related competitions.

This course provides students with an understanding of the aspects of human growth and development from prenatal to school age (0-5 years old). The content covers children’s physical, mental, emotional, and social growth from the prenatal period through aging. Parenting skills are developed as the appropriate care, positive guidance techniques, and child-related issues are studied. Learning activities, observation techniques, and working with children are included.

This year-long course prepares individuals for child-related careers and/or more extensive parenting skills through personal interaction with children. Instruction is given in developing positive relationships with and learning experiences for children, childcare policies and management, guidance techniques, and health and safety concerns. Onsite preschool and/or child care experiences will be a major component of the course. Previous completion of the Child Development course is required. FCCLA may be an integral part of the course.

Continuation of Early Childhood Education 1, join us as we investigate the unique learning years from 3-5. You will create hands-on activities and developmentally appropriate activities that will help you interact and teach preschool aged children.

Continuation of Early Childhood Education 1 & 2\. This is the next step in becoming a professional early childhood teacher. You will spend time in class and in the preschool developing your professional skills.

Have you ever thought about becoming a teacher? Or just wondered what happens on the other side of the desk? In this course we will dive into educational roles and opportunities, learning styles and stages, lesson planning and assessments, and student accommodations. So whether you’re dreaming of becoming the next legendary mentor or just want to better understand the profession that creates all other professions, this course is for you. Suggested prerequisite for Aspiring Educators CE.

Topics to be covered include gaming history, the development cycle, creating a story and flow for the game, determining perspective, creating assets, creating a user interface, and creating game mechanics (such as player control, collisions, scrolling, and animations).

courses.description: \\Full year can give UVU: CS 1400 Credit “I think everybody in this country should learn how to program a computer because it teaches you how to think,” said Steve Jobs. This year-long computer programming course will be fun and also teach you to think. This includes creating software, game development, and business applications. Students will learn to design, code, and test their own programs. Students will learn Microsoft’s C\# programming language, Kodu game development, and MonoGame studio. As a supplement and reinforcement, students will create basic PC games. This course includes the fundamentals and basic theories of computer programming, simple control and data structures, basic operating systems and file commands, and the use of text files. Students will also apply mathematical skills throughout the course. The course fundamentals include conditionals, looping, methods, file manipulation, arrays, objects, and classes.

courses.description: \\Concurrent Enrollment Credit UVU: CS 1410 Are you looking for a career in computer programming? Then this C\# and C++ course is a must! You should learn C++ because it's very commonly used, it's efficient, and it's the basis of many other languages in common use. This is an advanced course in computer programming using C\# and C++ which reviews and builds on the concepts introduced in CP1 and CP2. CP3 introduces students to more advanced concepts including classes, inheritance, polymorphism, dynamic allocation of data, advanced GUI techniques, and application of pointers, queues, stacks, and recursion. Students will learn to design, code, and test their own programs. As a course supplement, students will learn to create PC games using C\# and MonoGame Studio. This is a UVU Concurrent Enrollment course offering 3 credit hours: CS 1410\.

courses.description: Interior Design 1 is your introduction to how designers make spaces look great and work well. Through creative, hands-on projects, you’ll learn to use the elements and principles of design, experiment with color, and practice arranging furniture and décor so rooms feel balanced, comfortable, and functional. You’ll evaluate floor plans, try basic space-planning strategies, and learn simple professional display techniques. By the end of the semester, you’ll see everyday spaces in a totally new way — and you’ll take the Interior Design 1 CTE Skills Certification Test.

Ready to take your design skills to the next level? In this class, you'll dive deeper into the elements and principles of design while exploring the worlds of architecture, furniture styles, materials, color, textiles, lighting, and space planning. You’ll bring ideas to life through hands-on, creative projects—like redesigning rooms, choosing finishes and materials, planning realistic furniture layouts, experimenting with color and lighting, and making professional-style design boards. Interior Design 1 is recommended, but what you really need is curiosity, creativity, and a willingness to explore your own design style. You will take the Interior Design 2 CTE Skills Certification Test at the end of the semester.

Fashion Design Studio is a hands-on, creativity-driven course where you’ll explore how fashion shapes culture, self-expression, and everyday life. This class introduces you to the fashion industry while helping you develop your own design voice through artistic and practical projects. You’ll learn the fundamentals of fashion design, including the elements and principles of design, textiles and materials, trend influence, and smart consumer choices. Whether you love art, clothing, creativity, or are curious about careers in fashion, this class is for you!

Sewing Construction & Textiles 1 is a hands-on class where you’ll learn how to confidently use a sewing machine and create projects you’re proud of. You’ll practice reading patterns, choosing fabrics, and solving design problems while building real-life skills like patience, creativity, and responsibility. By the end of the class, you’ll understand how clothes and textiles are made — and you’ll leave with projects you created yourself. Students will take the Sewing 1 CTE Skills Certification Test at the end of the semester.

prerequisites: Sewing Construction & Textiles 1 OR Sports and Outdoor Design 1 (Sports Sewing 1\)

courses.description: Sewing Construction & Textiles 2 is where your sewing skills really level up. Building on what you learned in Sewing 1 (or Sports Sewing 1), you’ll work on more advanced projects while learning new techniques that make your sewing look clean, professional, and customized. You’ll practice using different fabrics, follow more detailed patterns, learn how to thread and use a serger, and try out specialty sewing feet and stitches to create polished finishes. You’ll gain confidence, speed, and the ability to problem-solve like a real designer. By the end of the semester, you’ll have skills you can use for hobbies, jobs, or future design classes — and you’ll take the Sewing 2 CTE Skills Certification Test.

courses.description: Sports Sewing 2 builds on what you learned in Sewing 1 or Sports & Outdoor Design 1 and takes your skills to the next level. In this class, you’ll focus on designing and constructing projects made with sportswear and outdoor fabrics — think performance materials, activewear details, and functional design. You’ll practice intermediate construction techniques, learn how to use and care for a serger, experiment with specialty sewing feet and stitches, and problem-solve as you create personalized projects. The projects are similar in skill level to Sewing 2, but the fabrics and designs are geared toward active, outdoor, and performance use. By the end of the semester, you’ll have confidence working with more technical fabrics and finishes — and you’ll take the Sports & Outdoor Design 2 CTE Skills Certification Test.

courses.description: Sewing Construction & Textiles 3 is an advanced course for highly motivated students who are serious about sewing and design. With teacher approval — and after completing Sewing 1 and Sewing 2 — you’ll dive into projects that feel more professional and career-focused. You’ll explore apparel design, pattern drafting, alterations, fitting, and advanced construction techniques, while also learning how textiles behave and how quality garments are created. You’ll connect sewing skills to real-world opportunities by exploring entrepreneurship, ethical production, and what it takes to run a small apparel business or side hustle. By the end of the semester, you’ll be ready to take the Sewing 3 CTE State Test and earn your Skills Certification.

This introduction course focuses on drawing and painting in a digital environment while developing communication and visual storytelling skills. Students use industry-standard software (Adobe Suite) to create character designs, concept art, and finished illustrations while learning about composition, color, perspective, shading, and foundational illustration techniques.

Ever wanted to take cool photos but don’t know where to start? This class will teach you the basics of using a camera, taking great pictures, and editing them to look amazing using Photoshop and Lightroom. Whether you want to capture fun moments or dream of becoming a photographer, this is the perfect place to start.

Note: Students will be required to have access to a film SLR camera and a Digital camera. Now it’s time to take your photography further. In this class, you’ll work on real projects, experiment with creative ideas, and build a collection of your best photos. You’ll learn how to make your pictures look more professional

Note: Students will be required to have access to a film SLR camera and a Digital camera. Let's step up your photography skills even more. This advanced class will help you master your camera, edit like a pro, and work on real-world projects. You’ll also start building a professional portfolio to show off your skills. If you’re thinking about a career in photography or just want to be amazing at it, this class will get you there!

This is a hands-on class where students work to make video content for Lehi Television, a student-led broadcasting program. Students work in a simulated professional (CTE) environment on projects intended to entertain and inform an audience of their peers. Students will utilize cameras, microphones, computers and editing software in the creation of skits, commercials, features, news packages, and other projects with the goal of informing our campus about events, organizations, and pieces of student-culture important to their lives as Lehi students. Students learn video editing, camera techniques, on-air performance, and how to work as part of a production team.

courses.description: This course introduces the concepts of object-oriented programming using Visual Basic.net. The class is presented with the tools, structure, syntax, and basic OOP design techniques for developing well-formed programs. Students study concepts such as classes, methods, fields, data types, control constructs, data I/O, exception handling, and class libraries. Students will write several programming assignments that demonstrate their understanding of OOP concepts.

courses.description: Web Development 1 is a course designed to guide students in a project-based environment in the development of up-to-date concepts and skills that are used in the development of today’s websites. Students will learn the fundamentals of how the Internet works. They will learn and use the basic building blocks of the World Wide Web: HTML5 coding, cascading style sheets (CSS), and JavaScript. They follow the steps to create a website by planning, designing, developing, deploying, and maintaining the website projects. Students will learn and use different scripting technologies to create more dynamic and interactive websites. They will learn what it takes for a career in web development as they complete projects and create their own website.
